11 min read
1 hour ago

--

What I learned auditing a Laravel loyalty platform that was slow for reasons no dashboard could show me

I was handed two problems in the same month.
The first was a complaint, not a ticket: the app feels slow. App launch took 2–3 seconds. The marketplace took 4–5. Applying a discount code took up to 7 seconds — long enough that users assumed it had failed and tapped again.
The second was a meeting invitation: plan the AWS → GCP database migration.
They looked like unrelated pieces of work. They were the same problem wearing two costumes, and the most valuable thing I did that month was notice that before the meeting started.
This is what I found, how I found it, and the two ideas I’d take to any codebase — the antipattern list, and the arithmetic that turned a migration plan into a prerequisite list.

Part 1: Scope the audit to what the client actually calls
The platform is a Laravel 10 backend for a loyalty and rewards app: earn/burn points, subscriptions, food ordering, wallet cashback, and a stack of payment gateway integrations. Layered and interface-driven — Route → Middleware → Controller → Service → Repository → Model. It’s a mature codebase with hundreds of endpoints across v1, v2, admin, B2B, and partner surfaces.
That maturity is exactly the problem. “The app is slow” against a codebase that size is not an actionable statement. You can profile everything and drown, or you can guess and be wrong.
I did neither. I started from the mobile client’s APIEndpoints contract — the definitive list of what the app actually calls — and audited only those endpoints. Everything else, however ugly, was out of scope.
This single constraint did more for the audit than any tool. It cut the surface area to something one person could trace exhaustively, and it guaranteed every finding mapped to a real user-visible symptom. An N+1 in an admin report nobody runs at peak is not why login takes 3 seconds.
Then I traced each endpoint by hand — controller to service to repository — and required every finding to cite file:line. Every claimed missing index got verified against database/migrations/ rather than assumed. The output was organized by app screen, not by module:
Launch / Home — /api/v1/login, /api/v1/phone/confirm, then a parallel fan-out: /api/v2/partners/followed, /api/v2/slider, /api/v2/home-section/voucher, /api/v2/home-section/offer, /api/v1/profile.
Organizing by screen is why this landed with people who don’t read PHP. “The launch fan-out is uncached” is a sentence a product manager can act on. “HomeSectionController lacks a cache layer" is not.

Part 2: Five antipatterns, not eighty findings
The audit surfaced dozens of individual issues. Presented as a list of eighty, it would have been ignored — a wall of complaints nobody can prioritize.
But they weren’t eighty problems. They were five mistakes made repeatedly, by different people, across years. That reframing is what made the work actionable.
1. External HTTP inside a database transaction
This is the seven-second discount code, and it’s the most expensive mistake in the codebase.
VoucherController::redeem opens a transaction, calls an external redemption API, waits, and commits. A lockForUpdate on the user row compounds it. For the entire duration of that third-party round trip, a database transaction is open and a row is locked.
The latency is bad. The failure mode is worse: when the vendor gets slow, transactions pile up holding locks, and connection pool exhaustion takes down endpoints that have nothing to do with vouchers. Your database’s health becomes a function of a third party’s uptime.
The rule that falls out: a transaction may hold locks, or it may wait on a network, but never both.
Fixing it isn’t just moving the call. Removing the transaction naively reintroduces a TOCTOU double-spend — two concurrent redemptions both reading a sufficient balance. The shape that works is an atomic debit first, external call second, refund on failure:
// Atomic debit before the external call: prevents TOCTOU double-spend
// across concurrent redemptions. DB::rollback() below refunds on failure.
if (!$this->user->debitCreditAtomic($user_id, (float) $voucher_price)) {
DB::rollback();
return response()->json(['error' => __('messages.not_enough_balance')], 405);
}Note what that comment does. It doesn’t say what the code does — you can read that. It records the invariant, so the next person doesn’t “simplify” the correctness away.
2. Outbound HTTP with no timeout
Pervasive. The search service, the marketplace client, EasyPromos, the analytics event pipeline, and most payment gateways all made outbound calls with no timeout configured.
No timeout doesn’t mean “wait a reasonable time.” It means wait as long as the OS default allows — which can be minutes. Every one of those is an unbounded pause with your user watching a spinner.
My favorite find in this category: one bank integration authenticated over HTTP in its service constructor. Not on first use — on instantiation. Every request to those endpoints paid an upfront round trip before doing anything, including requests that would fail validation immediately.
A default timeout policy is a few hours of work and the highest-leverage change on the list. It doesn’t make anything faster on a good day. It stops a vendor’s bad day from becoming yours.
3. N+1 in formatters and resources
The one with the widest blast radius: updateRetailersOfferPrice re-runs the user's loyalty-program and subscription lookups for every retailer set it touches — hit by retailer filter, retailer detail, collections, marketplace listing, and marketplace detail.
The user’s loyalty program doesn’t change during a request. It should be computed once and reused. Instead it’s recomputed per call site, per request, forever.
N+1s cluster in formatters and serializers for a structural reason worth internalizing: that’s where a developer holds a collection and a rich per-item shape at the same time. The loop feels local and harmless. Each iteration looks like one cheap query. The cost only exists in aggregate, and nothing in the file you’re reading tells you the collection has 200 items.
If you audit one layer of your codebase, audit the one that turns models into JSON.
4. Missing indexes on hot predicates
Verified against migrations, not guessed. The worst: the favorites table — read on essentially every retailer list and search — had no composite index at all, just plain foreign keys. inFavorite() ran per retailer, per page.
But I want to flag the caveat I put in the doc, because it’s the part people skip:
Before shipping any index, run EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the real query against a prod-sized dataset; the range column (created_at) goes last; *_transactions are write-heavy — add only indexes a hot read uses.
An index isn’t free. It’s a write-time tax paid forever to make reads faster. On append-heavy transaction tables, a speculative index is a permanent cost for a hypothetical gain. “Add an index” is a hypothesis, not a fix — EXPLAIN is how it becomes a fix.
5. No caching on the launch path
Every single app launch re-queried the entire v2 home fan-out from scratch. Sliders, home sections, followed partners — content that’s identical across thousands of users and changes maybe daily.
Meanwhile /api/v1/profile ran 8+ sequential queries with lazy-loaded relations, and a CheckUserBlocked middleware ran a user lookup on every authenticated request — multiplied across five parallel launch calls.

Part 3: The arithmetic that changed the meeting
Now the migration.
The meeting was titled “plan the AWS → GCP database migration.” The database tier would move to GCP; app servers would stay on AWS. Compliance was the stated driver.
Note what that title does: it assumes its own conclusion. It’s not “how do we meet the compliance requirement” — it’s “plan this specific solution to it.” The most useful thing I could contribute wasn’t a better plan. It was checking whether the premise held.
So the document opened with questions instead of phases:
1. Does compliance say “inside KSA” or “in-region”? Inside KSA → GCP Dammam works, AWS Bahrain doesn’t. In-region → AWS Bahrain works, and eu-west-1 → me-south-1 inside AWS is far cheaper and safer than cross-cloud. Legal decides this, not us.
One question, asked before the work rather than after, potentially eliminates the entire cross-cloud project. If “in-region” satisfies compliance, the answer is a region move inside AWS — dramatically less risk for the same compliance outcome. That question was cheap to ask and expensive to skip.
Then the arithmetic that connected the two projects:
Per our June audit: launch already 2–3s, marketplace 4–5s, discount-code 7s. The N+1s pay the RTT per query. At 15ms RTT, 200 sequential queries = 3s of round trips before anything executes. Fix before cutover, not after. This is arithmetic, not tuning.
This is the moment the two problems became one.
Today, an N+1 firing 200 queries against a database on the same local network is bad but survivable — sub-millisecond latency hides a multitude of sins. Move that database across a cloud boundary and every one of those queries pays real network RTT. The N+1 doesn’t get slightly worse. It gets multiplied by 15 milliseconds, 200 times over, before a single row is read.
Existing latency is a rate. Cross-cloud RTT is a multiplier on your query count. The N+1s weren’t a performance nit anymore — they were a migration blocker, and the audit had already located every one of them by file:line.
Two more blockers made the same point:
The cache was the database. Four app servers shared cache entries through MySQL. Redis was configured but unused. Move the database across clouds and every cache read becomes a cross-cloud round trip — the cache becomes the slow thing it exists to avoid.
TLS was hardcoded off. Across five connection blocks: MYSQL_ATTR_SSL_CA => '' and MYSQL_ATTR_SSL_VERIFY_SERVER_CERT => false. Not env-driven — a code change, not a config change. And VERIFY_SERVER_CERT => false means encryption without server authentication: MITM-vulnerable. Tolerable inside a VPC. Not across the public internet — which is precisely where this migration proposed to send that traffic.
That last one has a nasty irony worth sitting with. A migration justified by compliance would, without a code change nobody had scoped, have sent production data across the internet with authentication disabled. The compliance project was on track to create the compliance incident. Verify TLS before cutover, not after.

Part 4: Plans I’d trust vs. plans I wouldn’t
Two habits from the migration plan I’d defend in any review.
Define the rollback trigger in advance.
RDS stays untouched 2+ weeks. Rollback = stop app, revert three .env values, start. Rehearse it in Phase 2. Define the trigger in advance (e.g. p95 on /retailers/filter > Xms for 10 min) — deciding mid-incident goes badly.
A rollback plan you haven’t rehearsed is a wish. And a rollback trigger invented during an incident gets negotiated against sunk cost — three months of work makes “let’s give it another hour” sound reasonable at 2am. Write the number down while you’re calm.
Also worth naming: the one-way door. Once production writes land on the new database, rollback means losing them or reverse-syncing. The window is short by nature. Know that going in.
Production numbers come from a rehearsal, not from a guess. Phase 2 restores to dev, points dev at GCP, runs the test suite, measures endpoint latency against the June baselines as an explicit go/no-go, and times the restore. That’s where the downtime estimate comes from. Every downtime number I’ve seen produced any other way has been fiction.
And the recommendation I’d stand behind hardest:
DB-only is a step, not an end state. App in AWS + DB in GCP = permanent cross-cloud latency and egress. Fine as transition; hard to justify as architecture.
If it’s a transition, say so and name the destina
